questions.text: A political party is a group of people who come together to:
options: Contest elections and hold power in the government
questions.explanation: Political parties are organized groups that seek to influence or control government policy, usually by nominating candidates and contesting elections.

questions.text: Which of the following are the three components of a political party?
options: The leaders, active members, and followers
questions.explanation: Every political party has leaders who make decisions, active members who work for the party, and followers who support their ideology.

questions.text: Partisanship is a term used to describe:
options: A person who is strongly committed to a party, group or faction
questions.explanation: Partisanship is marked by a tendency to take a side and inability to take a balanced view on an issue.

questions.text: Which function of political parties involves choosing candidates to fight elections?
questions.explanation: In most democracies, elections are fought mainly among the candidates put up by political parties.

questions.text: Parties play a decisive role in making ________ for a country.
questions.explanation: Laws are debated and passed in the legislature, and since most members belong to a party, they follow the party line.

questions.text: The party which loses elections and does not form the government plays the role of:
questions.explanation: The opposition voices different views and criticizes the government for its failures or wrong policies.

questions.text: Why are political parties considered a necessity in a representative democracy?
options: To increase the number of candidates
To ensure every individual can vote
Because without parties, every candidate would be independent and no one could promise major policy changes
questions.explanation: Parties provide a mechanism to bring various representatives together so that a responsible government can be formed.

questions.text: In which country is a one-party system practiced?
questions.explanation: In China, only the Communist Party is allowed to rule, though legally people are free to form parties.

questions.text: A two-party system is prominently found in:
questions.explanation: In these countries, power usually alternates between two main parties, although other small parties may exist.

questions.text: If several parties compete for power and more than two parties have a reasonable chance of winning, it is called a:
questions.explanation: India has a multi-party system where many parties compete and often form coalitions.

questions.text: When several parties in a multi-party system join hands for the purpose of contesting elections and winning power, it is called an:
questions.explanation: Examples include the NDA (National Democratic Alliance) and the UPA (United Progressive Alliance) in India.

questions.text: The authority that registers and recognizes political parties in India is:
questions.explanation: Every party in the country has to register with the Election Commission, which offers special facilities to large and established parties.

questions.text: To be recognized as a 'National Party', a party must secure at least ________ percent of total votes in Lok Sabha or Assembly elections in four states.
questions.explanation: In addition to 6% votes, it must win at least four seats in the Lok Sabha.

questions.text: A 'State Party' must secure at least 6% of the total votes in an election to the Legislative Assembly and win at least:
questions.explanation: Securing 6% votes and winning 2 seats are the criteria for recognition as a State Party.

questions.text: Which of these is the oldest political party in India, founded in 1885?
questions.explanation: The Indian National Congress (INC) played a dominant role in Indian politics for many decades after independence.

questions.text: The Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) was formed in 1980 by reviving the erstwhile:
questions.explanation: Syama Prasad Mookerjee founded the Bharatiya Jana Sangh in 1951, which later became the BJP.

questions.text: The Bahujan Samaj Party (BSP) was formed in 1984 under the leadership of:
questions.explanation: BSP seeks to represent and secure power for the 'bahujan samaj' which includes Dalits, Adivasis, and OBCs.

questions.text: Which party believes in Marxism-Leninism and supports socialism, secularism and democracy?
questions.explanation: The Communist Party of India (Marxist) split from CPI in 1964 and has a strong base in West Bengal and Kerala.

questions.text: The symbol of the All India Trinamool Congress (AITC) is:
questions.explanation: AITC was launched in 1998 and became a national party in 2016.

questions.text: Which challenge to political parties refers to the concentration of power in the hands of one or few leaders at the top?
questions.explanation: Parties do not keep membership registers or hold regular organizational elections, leading to power being held by a few.

questions.text: Dynastic succession is a challenge because:
options: It prevents ordinary workers from rising to the top
questions.explanation: In many parties, the top positions are always controlled by members of one family.

questions.text: The use of money and muscle power in parties is especially prominent during:
questions.explanation: Parties tend to nominate candidates who can raise lots of money or use force to win votes.

questions.text: The decline in ideological differences between parties leads to the challenge of:
options: Lack of meaningful choice to voters
questions.explanation: Voters find it difficult to choose when parties offer very similar policies and programs.

questions.text: The Anti-Defection Law was passed to prevent elected MLAs and MPs from:
questions.explanation: Defection is changing party allegiance from the party on which a person got elected to a different party.

questions.text: The Supreme Court passed an order to reduce the influence of money and criminals by making it mandatory for candidates to file an:
questions.explanation: An affidavit gives details of property and criminal cases pending against the candidate.

questions.text: The Election Commission passed an order making it necessary for political parties to:
options: Hold organizational elections and file income tax returns
questions.explanation: This step was taken to ensure internal democracy and transparency in party funding.

questions.text: One suggested reform is to have a law to regulate the ________ affairs of political parties.
questions.explanation: This would make it compulsory for parties to maintain a register of members and follow their own constitution.

questions.text: Another suggestion for reform is that the state should contribute to:
questions.explanation: Government giving parties money for election support could help reduce the influence of wealthy donors.

questions.text: Political parties can be reformed if people ________ through petitions and agitations.
questions.explanation: Public pressure can force parties to take reforms seriously if they fear losing public support.

questions.text: Which party system is often criticized for leading to political instability?
questions.explanation: Coalition governments in multi-party systems can sometimes be unstable due to differing interests of partners.

questions.text: The 'Hindutva' or cultural nationalism is a core element of the ideology of:
questions.explanation: BJP wants to build a strong and modern India by drawing inspiration from India's ancient culture and values.

questions.text: A 'Coalition' government is formed when:
options: A single party wins a clear majority
No single party wins a majority and two or more parties join together
questions.explanation: Coalitions are a common feature of the multi-party system in India.

questions.text: The United Progressive Alliance (UPA) is led by which party?
questions.explanation: INC was the leading party of the UPA coalition which was in power from 2004 to 2014.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a recognized national party in India (as per the textbook context)?
questions.explanation: Shiromani Akali Dal is a state (regional) party primarily active in Punjab.

questions.text: The problem of 'Meaningful Choice' is that:
options: There is no difference in policies of various parties
Voters are not allowed to vote
questions.explanation: In recent years, there has been a decline in the ideological differences among parties in most parts of the world.

questions.text: An 'Active Member' of a party is one who:
options: Works for the party and participates in its activities
questions.explanation: Active members are the workers who maintain the party's presence at the grassroots level.

questions.text: The 'Lotus' is the election symbol of:
questions.explanation: The Lotus is the official symbol used by the Bharatiya Janata Party.

questions.text: Which party has a strong presence in the states of Punjab, West Bengal, Maharashtra and Andhra Pradesh (referring to NCP)?
questions.explanation: NCP was formed in 1999 following a split in the Congress party.

questions.text: The quality of democracy depends on the degree of ________ by people.
questions.explanation: Active participation of citizens is crucial for the healthy functioning of political parties and democracy.

questions.text: Which of these is a function of the opposition party?
questions.explanation: The opposition keeps the government in check by highlighting its failures and mobilization of opposition to the government.
